channel.title: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis (no repeats)
channel.description: Table of Contents for Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis. List of articles from both the latest and EarlyView issues.
channel.dc:title: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis (no repeats)

image.title: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Bifunctional Photocatalysts for Asymmetric Epoxidation of Chalcones
item.dc:description: Chiral epoxides are valuable intermediates widely used in pharmaceutical synthesis and functional materials. In this work, herein, we designed and developed a bifunctional chiral catalyst by grafting a photosensitizing unit onto chiral iron complexes of (R,R)−3,4‐diaminopyrrolidine‐derived N4 ligands. Employing molecular oxygen or air as a green oxidant and visible light as the driving force, this catalytic system successfully achieved asymmetric epoxidation of chalcones, furnishing the desired epoxides in high yields with excellent enantiopurity.
item.content:encoded: Chiral epoxides are valuable intermediates widely used in pharmaceutical synthesis and functional materials. In this work, herein, we designed and developed a bifunctional chiral catalyst by grafting a photosensitizing unit onto chiral iron complexes of (R,R)−3,4-diaminopyrrolidine-derived N4 ligands. Employing molecular oxygen or air as a green oxidant and visible light as the driving force, this catalytic system successfully achieved asymmetric epoxidation of chalcones, furnishing the desired epoxides in high yields with excellent enantiopurity.
item.dc:creator: Qun‐Qun Tian, Haopeng Yuan, Hong‐Yu Zhang, Yuecheng Zhang, Jiquan Zhao
item.dc:title: Bifunctional Photocatalysts for Asymmetric Epoxidation of Chalcones

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Front Cover: Unveiling Value in Plastic Waste: Synthesis of 1,3‐Dioxolan‐4‐ones from Polylactic Acid and Polyoxymethylene to Enhance Plastic Biodegradation (Adv. Synth. Catal. 7/2026)
item.dc:description: Plastic Biodegradation In their Research Article (10.1002/adsc.70354), Hye‐Young Jang and co‐workers report an efficient acid‐catalyzed strategy to upcycle PLA and POM waste into high‐value dioxolanones. Used as monomers, these derivatives create a PLLA‐based polymer with enhanced thermal stability and biodegradability that outperforms cellulose—a first for sustainable plastic design.
item.content:encoded:
Plastic Biodegradation

In their Research Article (10.1002/adsc.70354), Hye-Young Jang and co-workers report an efficient acid-catalyzed strategy to upcycle PLA and POM waste into high-value dioxolanones. Used as monomers, these derivatives create a PLLA-based polymer with enhanced thermal stability and biodegradability that outperforms cellulose—a first for sustainable plastic design.
item.dc:creator: Mi‐hyun Lee, Joohyun Park, Hyeongyeong Lee, Woo Yeon Cho, Semin Son, Pyung Cheon Lee, In‐Hwan Lee, Hye‐Young Jang
item.dc:title: Front Cover: Unveiling Value in Plastic Waste: Synthesis of 1,3‐Dioxolan‐4‐ones from Polylactic Acid and Polyoxymethylene to Enhance Plastic Biodegradation (Adv. Synth. Catal. 7/2026)

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Interface‐Aware Design of Biocatalysis
item.dc:description:
Interfaces impact biocatalysis scalability. We reframe gas–liquid, liquid–liquid, and solid–liquid boundaries as design variables, specific interfacial area and renewal rate, that govern activation or deactivation. Mechanistic evidence, quantification tools, and reactor/material strategies enable longer lifetimes and higher integrated productivity by separating mass transfer benefits from interfacial damage.
Interfaces play a critical role in biocatalysis but are rarely considered during biocatalysis process development. Gas–liquid, liquid–liquid, and solid–liquid interfaces are present in almost every industrial application, from aerated oxidations to aqueous‐organic biphasic reactions and immobilized systems. Interfaces therefore can severely reduce catalyst lifetime. Oxidoreductases that are stable for tens of hours in solution may lose activity within minutes under air sparging, while whole‐cell systems exposed to organic solvents may show productivity shifts that correlate with the exposure to interfacial area. Yet interfaces are not always detrimental. Lipases, for instance, require interfacial activation to function, Pickering emulsions and colloidosomes can enhance both stability and turnover, and hybrid bio‐abiotic assemblies exploit interfaces to access new reactivity. The challenge is to recognize interfaces as measurable variables defined by interfacial area, renewal rate, and adsorption, rather than unpredictable side effects. In this perspective, we discuss the importance of recognizing interfaces during biocatalysis process design. We also highlight case studies where the outcomes depend on interfacial control and evaluate molecular, material, and reactor strategies to mitigate or exploit interface phenomena. By incorporating measurement of interfacial stability into standard workflows, we can transform interfaces from silent killers into controllable variables that affect productivity, cost, and sustainability in biocatalysis processes.
item.content:encoded:
Interfaces impact biocatalysis scalability. We reframe gas–liquid, liquid–liquid, and solid–liquid boundaries as design variables, specific interfacial area and renewal rate, that govern activation or deactivation. Mechanistic evidence, quantification tools, and reactor/material strategies enable longer lifetimes and higher integrated productivity by separating mass transfer benefits from interfacial damage.


Interfaces play a critical role in biocatalysis but are rarely considered during biocatalysis process development. Gas–liquid, liquid–liquid, and solid–liquid interfaces are present in almost every industrial application, from aerated oxidations to aqueous-organic biphasic reactions and immobilized systems. Interfaces therefore can severely reduce catalyst lifetime. Oxidoreductases that are stable for tens of hours in solution may lose activity within minutes under air sparging, while whole-cell systems exposed to organic solvents may show productivity shifts that correlate with the exposure to interfacial area. Yet interfaces are not always detrimental. Lipases, for instance, require interfacial activation to function, Pickering emulsions and colloidosomes can enhance both stability and turnover, and hybrid bio-abiotic assemblies exploit interfaces to access new reactivity. The challenge is to recognize interfaces as measurable variables defined by interfacial area, renewal rate, and adsorption, rather than unpredictable side effects. In this perspective, we discuss the importance of recognizing interfaces during biocatalysis process design. We also highlight case studies where the outcomes depend on interfacial control and evaluate molecular, material, and reactor strategies to mitigate or exploit interface phenomena. By incorporating measurement of interfacial stability into standard workflows, we can transform interfaces from silent killers into controllable variables that affect productivity, cost, and sustainability in biocatalysis processes.
item.dc:creator: Ariadna Pié Porta, Maria Isabel Rodriguez‐Torres, Christoffer Rischel Hilbert, John M. Woodley, Elif Erdem
item.dc:title: Interface‐Aware Design of Biocatalysis

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis, Characterization, and Catalytic Applications of Zeolite‐Based Composites Integrating LDHs, MOFs, and Polymers
item.dc:description: Zeolite‐based composites integrating with layered double hydroxides, metal–organic frameworks, and polymers have emerged as versatile catalysts combining multifunctional active sites. This review provides an overview of their synthesis, characterization, and catalytic applications, highlighting how rational design governs catalytic behavior. We outline available approaches for engineering these composite catalysts, including in situ growth, surface functionalization, and core–shell assembly. In addition, the key characterization techniques are summarized to elucidate structure–activity correlations and to verify the integrity of hybrid architectures such as X‐ray diffraction, N2 sorption, transmission electron microscopy, X‐ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and solid‐state NMR. Moreover, the examples of their catalytic performances have been demonstrated through several catalytic reactions, such as biomass conversion, CO2 utilization, and cascade/aldol condensation, driven by synergistic acid–base or redox interfaces or highly dispersed metal active sites derived from the hydride structure. Finally, we outline mechanistic insights, key challenges, and perspectives for designing durable, scalable, and multifunctional zeolite‐based composites toward sustainable catalytic applications.
item.content:encoded: Zeolite-based composites integrating with layered double hydroxides, metal–organic frameworks, and polymers have emerged as versatile catalysts combining multifunctional active sites. This review provides an overview of their synthesis, characterization, and catalytic applications, highlighting how rational design governs catalytic behavior. We outline available approaches for engineering these composite catalysts, including in situ growth, surface functionalization, and core–shell assembly. In addition, the key characterization techniques are summarized to elucidate structure–activity correlations and to verify the integrity of hybrid architectures such as X-ray diffraction, N2 sorption, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and solid-state NMR. Moreover, the examples of their catalytic performances have been demonstrated through several catalytic reactions, such as biomass conversion, CO2 utilization, and cascade/aldol condensation, driven by synergistic acid–base or redox interfaces or highly dispersed metal active sites derived from the hydride structure. Finally, we outline mechanistic insights, key challenges, and perspectives for designing durable, scalable, and multifunctional zeolite-based composites toward sustainable catalytic applications.
item.dc:creator: Warot Prasanseang, Chularat Wattanakit
item.dc:title: Synthesis, Characterization, and Catalytic Applications of Zeolite‐Based Composites Integrating LDHs, MOFs, and Polymers

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Transition‐Metal‐Free or Visible‐Light Catalyzed Selective C–C Bond Cleavage of Cyclobutanone Oxime Derivatives
item.dc:description:
This review summarizes recent advances in transition‐metal‐free and visible‐light‐catalyzed selective C–C bond cleavage of cyclobutanone oxime derivatives. It focuses on applications in cyanoalkylation, cyanoalkylsulfonylation, cyanoalkylcarbonylation, and multicomponent cyclizations, with discussions on mechanisms, substrate scope, and future prospects.
The cyanoalkyl group, as a key functional motif, is widely distributed in pharmacologically active molecules, natural products, and functional materials, and its efficient incorporation is of great significance for modern synthetic chemistry. Traditional cyanoalkylation methods usually rely on toxic cyanide sources, high temperatures, or transition‐metal catalysis, facing challenges such as harsh conditions and insufficient selectivity. In recent years, cyclobutanone oxime esters have emerged as ideal precursors for cyanoalkylation reactions due to their inherent ring strain and reactivity. Through visible‐light or transition‐metal‐free catalytic systems, they provide innovative pathways for the green and efficient construction of cyanoalkyl groups. This review systematically summarizes recent key advances in this field, covering C–C bond cleavage reactions of cyclobutanone oxime esters under visible‐light and transition‐metal‐free catalysis, with a focus on their applications in cyanoalkylation, cyanoalkylsulfonylation, cyanoalkylcarbonylation, and multicomponent cyclization transformations. Additionally, a detailed discussion will be provided on the substrate generality, mechanisms, and applications of these reactions, concluding with an outlook on the future development and current limitations of this research area.
item.content:encoded:
This review summarizes recent advances in transition-metal-free and visible-light-catalyzed selective C–C bond cleavage of cyclobutanone oxime derivatives. It focuses on applications in cyanoalkylation, cyanoalkylsulfonylation, cyanoalkylcarbonylation, and multicomponent cyclizations, with discussions on mechanisms, substrate scope, and future prospects.


The cyanoalkyl group, as a key functional motif, is widely distributed in pharmacologically active molecules, natural products, and functional materials, and its efficient incorporation is of great significance for modern synthetic chemistry. Traditional cyanoalkylation methods usually rely on toxic cyanide sources, high temperatures, or transition-metal catalysis, facing challenges such as harsh conditions and insufficient selectivity. In recent years, cyclobutanone oxime esters have emerged as ideal precursors for cyanoalkylation reactions due to their inherent ring strain and reactivity. Through visible-light or transition-metal-free catalytic systems, they provide innovative pathways for the green and efficient construction of cyanoalkyl groups. This review systematically summarizes recent key advances in this field, covering C–C bond cleavage reactions of cyclobutanone oxime esters under visible-light and transition-metal-free catalysis, with a focus on their applications in cyanoalkylation, cyanoalkylsulfonylation, cyanoalkylcarbonylation, and multicomponent cyclization transformations. Additionally, a detailed discussion will be provided on the substrate generality, mechanisms, and applications of these reactions, concluding with an outlook on the future development and current limitations of this research area.
item.dc:creator: Dong Zou, Jiaqi Yao
item.dc:title: Transition‐Metal‐Free or Visible‐Light Catalyzed Selective C–C Bond Cleavage of Cyclobutanone Oxime Derivatives

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: PhICl2/KSeCN‐Mediated Cascade Synthesis of 3,3′‐Diindolyl Selenides From 2‐Alkynylanilines
item.dc:description:
Symmetrical 3,3′‐diindolyl selenides were efficiently synthesized through a one‐pot reaction of 2‐alkynylanilines with PhICl2 and KSeCN under metal‐free conditions. This transformation proceeds via a dual electrophilic cascade involving in situ generated selenocyanogen chloride species, while a complementary protocol enables access to unsymmetrical analogues.
The reaction of 2‐alkynylanilines with KSeCN enabled by PhICl2 in anhydrous MeCN was found to undergo an alternative pathway distinct from the previous report, furnishing 3,3′‐diindolyl selenides in moderate to good yield. The mechanistic studies support a cascade process involving the formation of the electrophilic selenocyanogen chloride from the reaction of PhICl2 and KSeCN, electrophilic cyclization/selenocyanation of 2‐alkynylanilines to forge 3‐selenocyanato indole nuclei, followed by its further reaction with 2‐alkynylanilines to give the title 3,3′‐diindolyl selenides.
item.content:encoded:
Symmetrical 3,3′-diindolyl selenides were efficiently synthesized through a one-pot reaction of 2-alkynylanilines with PhICl2 and KSeCN under metal-free conditions. This transformation proceeds via a dual electrophilic cascade involving in situ generated selenocyanogen chloride species, while a complementary protocol enables access to unsymmetrical analogues.


The reaction of 2-alkynylanilines with KSeCN enabled by PhICl2 in anhydrous MeCN was found to undergo an alternative pathway distinct from the previous report, furnishing 3,3′-diindolyl selenides in moderate to good yield. The mechanistic studies support a cascade process involving the formation of the electrophilic selenocyanogen chloride from the reaction of PhICl2 and KSeCN, electrophilic cyclization/selenocyanation of 2-alkynylanilines to forge 3-selenocyanato indole nuclei, followed by its further reaction with 2-alkynylanilines to give the title 3,3′-diindolyl selenides.
item.dc:creator: Hui Zhao, Lingzhi Xu, Haofeng Shi, Shuoshuo Zhang, Yiran Zhou, Yingjie Gao, Yunfei Du
item.dc:title: PhICl2/KSeCN‐Mediated Cascade Synthesis of 3,3′‐Diindolyl Selenides From 2‐Alkynylanilines

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Sol‐Gel Spin‐Coated Pd‐ZIF67 Hybrid Catalytic Membranes for Allylic Arylation
item.dc:description:
This study employs a gel spin‐coating method to composite amorphous ZIF67 colloid onto a ceramic membrane support, followed by palladium impregnation, successfully preparing a novel palladium‐based catalytic membrane (Pd‐ZIF67@CM). The catalytic membrane enables efficient allylic arylation reactions in a flow membrane reactor and exhibits excellent catalytic activity under continuous‐flow conditions. Furthermore, the membrane catalyst demonstrates good stability and recyclablility.
A palladium‐incorporated ZIF‐67 catalytic membrane (Pd‐ZIF67@CM) is first fabricated by spin‐coating an amorphous ZIF‐67 colloid precursor onto a ceramic support. The resulting composite membrane demonstrates efficient and recyclable catalysis for continuous‐flow allylic arylation, exhibiting a broad substrate scope and retaining its activity over five consecutive cycles under mild conditions.
item.content:encoded:
This study employs a gel spin-coating method to composite amorphous ZIF67 colloid onto a ceramic membrane support, followed by palladium impregnation, successfully preparing a novel palladium-based catalytic membrane (Pd-ZIF67@CM). The catalytic membrane enables efficient allylic arylation reactions in a flow membrane reactor and exhibits excellent catalytic activity under continuous-flow conditions. Furthermore, the membrane catalyst demonstrates good stability and recyclablility.


A palladium-incorporated ZIF-67 catalytic membrane (Pd-ZIF67@CM) is first fabricated by spin-coating an amorphous ZIF-67 colloid precursor onto a ceramic support. The resulting composite membrane demonstrates efficient and recyclable catalysis for continuous-flow allylic arylation, exhibiting a broad substrate scope and retaining its activity over five consecutive cycles under mild conditions.
item.dc:creator: Yong Zhou, Lianzeng Zhang, Jiacheng Rui, Liang Shen, Rizhi Chen, Xiaojin Wu
item.dc:title: Sol‐Gel Spin‐Coated Pd‐ZIF67 Hybrid Catalytic Membranes for Allylic Arylation

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Photoinduced Electron Donor–Acceptor Complex‐Enabled Tandem Perfluoroalkylation/Cyclization of Internal Alkenes Toward Ring‐Fused Quinazolinones
item.dc:description: N‐(3‐Methylbut‐2‐en‐1‐yl)quinazolin‐4(3H)‐ones were designed as novel skeletons to provide C2‐perfluoroalkylated pyrrolo‐quinazolin‐4(3H)‐ones in moderate to excellent yields through electron donor–acceptor (EDA) complex‐triggered tandem perfluoroalkylation/cyclization with commercially available perfluoroalkyl iodides. Moreover, the construction of C3‐perfluoroalkylated piperidino‐quinazolinones was also achieved through the EDA complex‐promoted perfluoroalkylation/cyclization of N‐(4‐methylpent‐3‐en‐1‐yl)quinazolin‐4(3H)‐ones. In those reactions, the perfluoroalkyl group was directly installed into the heterocyclic ring cores instead of the side chains.
item.content:encoded: N-(3-Methylbut-2-en-1-yl)quinazolin-4(3H)-ones were designed as novel skeletons to provide C2-perfluoroalkylated pyrrolo-quinazolin-4(3H)-ones in moderate to excellent yields through electron donor–acceptor (EDA) complex-triggered tandem perfluoroalkylation/cyclization with commercially available perfluoroalkyl iodides. Moreover, the construction of C3-perfluoroalkylated piperidino-quinazolinones was also achieved through the EDA complex-promoted perfluoroalkylation/cyclization of N-(4-methylpent-3-en-1-yl)quinazolin-4(3H)-ones. In those reactions, the perfluoroalkyl group was directly installed into the heterocyclic ring cores instead of the side chains.
item.dc:creator: Sheng Yu, Guocheng Yin, Jin‐Tao Yu
item.dc:title: Photoinduced Electron Donor–Acceptor Complex‐Enabled Tandem Perfluoroalkylation/Cyclization of Internal Alkenes Toward Ring‐Fused Quinazolinones

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Advances in the Synthesis of Levetiracetam: Classical Approaches and Patented Innovations
item.dc:description:
Levetiracetam, a widely used antiepileptic drug, features a unique mechanism and favorable safety profile. This review traces the evolution of its synthesis, from early routes to modern strategies enhancing yield, cost‐efficiency, and environmental sustainability. Key publications and patents are examined, highlighting industrial progress and research advances, and outlining future opportunities in levetiracetam manufacture and synthesis of related compounds.
Levetiracetam, a widely used antiepileptic drug, is notable for its unique mechanism and favorable safety profile. This review summarizes the evolution of its synthesis, from early methods to recent innovations aimed at improving yield, cost‐efficiency, and environmental impact. Key publications and patents are discussed, highlighting industrial advancements and research progress. The article serves as a valuable resource for professionals in pharmaceutical synthesis and explores future directions in levetiracetam production and related compounds.
item.content:encoded:
Levetiracetam, a widely used antiepileptic drug, features a unique mechanism and favorable safety profile. This review traces the evolution of its synthesis, from early routes to modern strategies enhancing yield, cost-efficiency, and environmental sustainability. Key publications and patents are examined, highlighting industrial progress and research advances, and outlining future opportunities in levetiracetam manufacture and synthesis of related compounds.


Levetiracetam, a widely used antiepileptic drug, is notable for its unique mechanism and favorable safety profile. This review summarizes the evolution of its synthesis, from early methods to recent innovations aimed at improving yield, cost-efficiency, and environmental impact. Key publications and patents are discussed, highlighting industrial advancements and research progress. The article serves as a valuable resource for professionals in pharmaceutical synthesis and explores future directions in levetiracetam production and related compounds.
item.dc:creator: Murugesan Sivaprakash, Kothapalli Bannoth Tejaswini, Kiran Kumar Pulukuri, Purushothaman Gopinath, Ekambaram Balaraman
item.dc:title: Advances in the Synthesis of Levetiracetam: Classical Approaches and Patented Innovations

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: A Scalable and Resource‐Saving Synthesis of Dipyrromethanes and Tetrapyrroles via Geminal Diethers: Minimal Polymer Formation and Facile Purification
item.dc:description:
Geminal dimethoxyethers react with pyrrolic nucleophiles in an efficient and clean reaction under anhydrous, mild conditions, which contrasts with the reactivity of the respective carbonyl compounds. Polymerization is effectively inhibited, providing access to dipyrromethanes, porphyrins, and calix[4]pyrroles that are easily purified. The method has a broad functional group tolerance and was scaled up to 500 mmol.
The reported syntheses of dipyrromethanes and tetrapyrroles suffer from accompanying polymerization processes lowering yields and necessitating a laborious workup. Here we present the reaction of geminal dimethoxyethers (i.e., acetals or ketals) with pyrrolic nucleophiles under mild conditions solving those problems. The observed minimal polymerization enables greatly simplified purification procedures. This is possible, because under anhydrous, mildly acidic conditions, geminal dimethoxyethers are more reactive than their carbonyl counterparts. The reaction of geminal dimethoxyethers with pyrrolic nucleophiles proceeds smoothly and selectively at 0°C. Computational studies suggest that geminal dimethoxyethers react readily, while unfeasibly high intermediate energies hamper the reaction of carbonyl compounds under the same conditions. We demonstrate a high functional group tolerance and high yields by the preparation of 27 dipyrromethanes, one porphyrin, and 20 calix[4]pyrroles, many of which include synthetic anchor points for facile postfunctionalization. The method was scaled up to a 500 mmol scale for a dipyrromethane and a 252 mmol scale for a two‐walled calix[4]pyrrole, without loss in yield or purity.
item.content:encoded:
Geminal dimethoxyethers react with pyrrolic nucleophiles in an efficient and clean reaction under anhydrous, mild conditions, which contrasts with the reactivity of the respective carbonyl compounds. Polymerization is effectively inhibited, providing access to dipyrromethanes, porphyrins, and calix[4]pyrroles that are easily purified. The method has a broad functional group tolerance and was scaled up to 500 mmol.


The reported syntheses of dipyrromethanes and tetrapyrroles suffer from accompanying polymerization processes lowering yields and necessitating a laborious workup. Here we present the reaction of geminal dimethoxyethers (i.e., acetals or ketals) with pyrrolic nucleophiles under mild conditions solving those problems. The observed minimal polymerization enables greatly simplified purification procedures. This is possible, because under anhydrous, mildly acidic conditions, geminal dimethoxyethers are more reactive than their carbonyl counterparts. The reaction of geminal dimethoxyethers with pyrrolic nucleophiles proceeds smoothly and selectively at 0°C. Computational studies suggest that geminal dimethoxyethers react readily, while unfeasibly high intermediate energies hamper the reaction of carbonyl compounds under the same conditions. We demonstrate a high functional group tolerance and high yields by the preparation of 27 dipyrromethanes, one porphyrin, and 20 calix[4]pyrroles, many of which include synthetic anchor points for facile postfunctionalization. The method was scaled up to a 500 mmol scale for a dipyrromethane and a 252 mmol scale for a two-walled calix[4]pyrrole, without loss in yield or purity.
item.dc:creator: Janos Wasternack, Clara Schritt, Luca Steiner, Ole Specht, Friedrich Braune, Marius Balizs, Sebastian Müller, Anja Wiesner, Beate Paulus, Siegfried Eigler, Christoph A. Schalley
item.dc:title: A Scalable and Resource‐Saving Synthesis of Dipyrromethanes and Tetrapyrroles via Geminal Diethers: Minimal Polymer Formation and Facile Purification

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Solvent‐Free Organocatalytic Synthesis of Fluorescent N‐Alkylated Pyridones: A Metal/Base‐Free Migration Strategy
item.dc:description:
An organocatalytic system employing Ph3CBr or TMSI enables efficient O‐ to N‐migration for the synthesis of N‐alkylated pyridones under solvent‐, transition‐metal‐, and base‐free conditions. This transformation exhibits remarkable substrate generality, successfully accommodating diverse functional groups including allyl, benzyl, propargyl, and vinyl moieties. Linear allyl pyridyl ethers undergo [1, 3,]‐migratory rearrangement, whereas their branched counterparts consistently proceed via [3, 3]‐migratory rearrangement. Stereoselective [1, 3]‐rearrangements predominantly preserve alkene geometry, while the resultant pyridones display tunable fluorescence (Φ up to 0.39 in chloroform). This protocol establishes an eco‐compatible and directly scalable platform for accessing functionalized heterocycles.
A solvent‐, transition‐metal‐, and base‐free organocatalytic system (Ph3CBr or TMSI) enables efficient O‐ to N‐migration for N‐alkylated pyridone synthesis. Critically, gram‐scale production of a cinnamyl‐derived product (4.73 mmol) completes within 3 h at 100°C without yield erosion. Stereospecific [1,3]‐rearrangements predominantly preserve alkene geometry, while resultant pyridones exhibit tunable fluorescence (Φ up to 0.39 in chloroform). This protocol establishes an eco‐compatible, directly amplifiable platform toward functional heterocycles.
item.content:encoded:
An organocatalytic system employing Ph3CBr or TMSI enables efficient O- to N-migration for the synthesis of N-alkylated pyridones under solvent-, transition-metal-, and base-free conditions. This transformation exhibits remarkable substrate generality, successfully accommodating diverse functional groups including allyl, benzyl, propargyl, and vinyl moieties. Linear allyl pyridyl ethers undergo [1, 3,]-migratory rearrangement, whereas their branched counterparts consistently proceed via [3, 3]-migratory rearrangement. Stereoselective [1, 3]-rearrangements predominantly preserve alkene geometry, while the resultant pyridones display tunable fluorescence (Φ up to 0.39 in chloroform). This protocol establishes an eco-compatible and directly scalable platform for accessing functionalized heterocycles.


A solvent-, transition-metal-, and base-free organocatalytic system (Ph3CBr or TMSI) enables efficient O- to N-migration for N-alkylated pyridone synthesis. Critically, gram-scale production of a cinnamyl-derived product (4.73 mmol) completes within 3 h at 100°C without yield erosion. Stereospecific [1,3]-rearrangements predominantly preserve alkene geometry, while resultant pyridones exhibit tunable fluorescence (Φ up to 0.39 in chloroform). This protocol establishes an eco-compatible, directly amplifiable platform toward functional heterocycles.
item.dc:creator: An Yan, Tong Han, Futian Mi, Turghun Muhammad, Dong Wang
item.dc:title: Solvent‐Free Organocatalytic Synthesis of Fluorescent N‐Alkylated Pyridones: A Metal/Base‐Free Migration Strategy

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Divergent Access to Fluorinated Pharmacons From Ethyl Trifluoropyruvate
item.dc:description:
Fluoro(oxo) functional group diversification is traditionally accomplished through either specialized reagents or nucleophilic trifluoromethylation. Herein is disclosed the use of commercially available building blocks for the synthesis of trifluoromethyl(hydroxy) esters. These esters can then be activated for the divergent synthesis of fluorinated pharmacons.
Fluoro(oxo) functional groups, such as trifluoromethyl ketones, trifluoroethanols, and difluoromethyl ketones, have emerged as desirable motifs in medicinal chemistry. Traditionally, the installation of these functional groups is accomplished through the use of either designer reagents or nucleophilic trifluoromethylation. In contrast, commercially available fluorinated building blocks, such as ethyl trifluoropyruvate, can be used in a divergent manner to access a wide variety of fluoro(oxo) functional groups. Herein, we demonstrate facile activation of trifluoromethyl(hydroxy) esters for the divergent synthesis of fluorinated pharmacons.
item.content:encoded:
Fluoro(oxo) functional group diversification is traditionally accomplished through either specialized reagents or nucleophilic trifluoromethylation. Herein is disclosed the use of commercially available building blocks for the synthesis of trifluoromethyl(hydroxy) esters. These esters can then be activated for the divergent synthesis of fluorinated pharmacons.


Fluoro(oxo) functional groups, such as trifluoromethyl ketones, trifluoroethanols, and difluoromethyl ketones, have emerged as desirable motifs in medicinal chemistry. Traditionally, the installation of these functional groups is accomplished through the use of either designer reagents or nucleophilic trifluoromethylation. In contrast, commercially available fluorinated building blocks, such as ethyl trifluoropyruvate, can be used in a divergent manner to access a wide variety of fluoro(oxo) functional groups. Herein, we demonstrate facile activation of trifluoromethyl(hydroxy) esters for the divergent synthesis of fluorinated pharmacons.
item.dc:creator: Rifat N. Nabi, Ryan C. Kashatus, Kaden M. Joseph, Salina Zhang, Daniel K. Kim
item.dc:title: Divergent Access to Fluorinated Pharmacons From Ethyl Trifluoropyruvate

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Liquid‐Assisted Grinding in Medicinal Mechanochemistry: A Pathway to Enhanced Reactivity and Selectivity
item.dc:description: Liquid‐assisted grinding (LAG) has emerged as a powerful and sustainable strategy in medicinal mechanochemistry, enabling the rapid, selective, and solvent‐minimized synthesis of pharmaceuticals and drug‐like molecules. This review systematically examines the transformative potential of LAG in constructing pharmacologically relevant architectures, highlighting its consistent superiority over both neat grinding and conventional solution‐based approaches, particularly in enhancing reactivity and exerting precise control over chemo‐, regio‐, stereo‐ and enantioselectivity. Central to this discussion is the multifaceted role of the liquid additives, which function not merely as processing aids but as dynamic mediators that modulate the microenvironment, acting as ligands or cocatalysts, functional reagents, and stabilizers to direct selectivity. The review concludes by addressing current challenges and outlining strategic pathways to advance LAG toward scalable, greener, and industrially viable pharmaceutical synthesis.
item.content:encoded: Liquid-assisted grinding (LAG) has emerged as a powerful and sustainable strategy in medicinal mechanochemistry, enabling the rapid, selective, and solvent-minimized synthesis of pharmaceuticals and drug-like molecules. This review systematically examines the transformative potential of LAG in constructing pharmacologically relevant architectures, highlighting its consistent superiority over both neat grinding and conventional solution-based approaches, particularly in enhancing reactivity and exerting precise control over chemo-, regio-, stereo- and enantioselectivity. Central to this discussion is the multifaceted role of the liquid additives, which function not merely as processing aids but as dynamic mediators that modulate the microenvironment, acting as ligands or cocatalysts, functional reagents, and stabilizers to direct selectivity. The review concludes by addressing current challenges and outlining strategic pathways to advance LAG toward scalable, greener, and industrially viable pharmaceutical synthesis.
item.dc:creator: Yuxin Fang, Jingbo Yu
item.dc:title: Liquid‐Assisted Grinding in Medicinal Mechanochemistry: A Pathway to Enhanced Reactivity and Selectivity

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Manganese(III)/Lewis Acid–Catalyzed Synthesis of β‐Hydroxysulfones
item.dc:description:
A simple manganese(III) salt with Lewis acid was employed as the catalyst to activate sodium sulfinate for its coupling with olefin toward β‐hydroxysulfone synthesis through aerobic oxidation.
An efficient method is developed for the synthesis of β‐hydroxysulfones from difunctionalized olefins and substituted sodium sulfinates with Mn(OAc)3/Lewis acid catalyst through aerobic oxidation, which provides β‐hydroxysulfones in middle to high yields with broad compatibility of functional groups. The high efficiency is attributed to the interaction between the Mn(III) species and Lewis acid such as Ce(OTf)3 that enhances its redox potential, thus accelerating Mn(III)‐catalyzed sulfonyl radical formation from sulfonates through single‐electron transfer mechanism.
item.content:encoded:
A simple manganese(III) salt with Lewis acid was employed as the catalyst to activate sodium sulfinate for its coupling with olefin toward β-hydroxysulfone synthesis through aerobic oxidation.


An efficient method is developed for the synthesis of β-hydroxysulfones from difunctionalized olefins and substituted sodium sulfinates with Mn(OAc)3/Lewis acid catalyst through aerobic oxidation, which provides β-hydroxysulfones in middle to high yields with broad compatibility of functional groups. The high efficiency is attributed to the interaction between the Mn(III) species and Lewis acid such as Ce(OTf)3 that enhances its redox potential, thus accelerating Mn(III)-catalyzed sulfonyl radical formation from sulfonates through single-electron transfer mechanism.
item.dc:creator: Shuang‐Long Li, Wenbo Lv, Zhuqi Chen, Zhenxing Wang, Guochuan Yin
item.dc:title: Manganese(III)/Lewis Acid–Catalyzed Synthesis of β‐Hydroxysulfones

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Direct Introduction of Fluorocyclopropyl Groups via Energy Transfer Catalysis
item.dc:description:
This work describes an efficient, straightforward protocol for the direct incorporation of fluorinated cyclopropyl moieties via energy‐transfer catalysis. The method unlocks access to a broad range of previously inaccessible fluorocyclopropyl compounds in good yields. Mechanistic insights, supported by DFT calculations, elucidate the reactivity and guide further synthetic applications.
This study presents an efficient and straightforward method for the direct incorporation of fluorinated cyclopropyl moieties via energy‐transfer catalysis. The approach leverages the reactivity of a diverse range of oxime esters functionalized with fluorinated cyclopropyl groups, enabling the selective formation of the desired products under mild conditions.
item.content:encoded:
This work describes an efficient, straightforward protocol for the direct incorporation of fluorinated cyclopropyl moieties via energy-transfer catalysis. The method unlocks access to a broad range of previously inaccessible fluorocyclopropyl compounds in good yields. Mechanistic insights, supported by DFT calculations, elucidate the reactivity and guide further synthetic applications.


This study presents an efficient and straightforward method for the direct incorporation of fluorinated cyclopropyl moieties via energy-transfer catalysis. The approach leverages the reactivity of a diverse range of oxime esters functionalized with fluorinated cyclopropyl groups, enabling the selective formation of the desired products under mild conditions.
item.dc:creator: Yinpeng Wang, Tao Yang, Marc Taillefer, Alexis Prieto
item.dc:title: Direct Introduction of Fluorocyclopropyl Groups via Energy Transfer Catalysis

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Unveiling Value in Plastic Waste: Synthesis of 1,3‐Dioxolan‐4‐ones from Polylactic Acid and Polyoxymethylene to Enhance Plastic Biodegradation
item.dc:description:
An acid‐catalyzed process provides a robust method to upcycle even impure and colored PLA and POM plastic waste into high‐value DOXs. Incorporating these DOXs enables the synthesis of PLLA materials that exhibit enhanced biodegradability, surpassing the performance of cellulose. This work establishes a sustainable chemical pathway and marks a significant step toward advanced materials.
Catalytic plastic upcycling protocols are essential for resolving the environmental and economic challenges associated with massive plastic production and disposal, as they convert complex waste streams into value‐added chemicals. We report an efficient acid‐catalyzed upcycling strategy for transforming poly(lactic acid) (PLA) and polyoxymethylene (POM) plastic waste. This innovative process directly yielding dioxolanones (DOXs) can be extended to use POM with various naturally abundant α‐hydroxy acids. This work significantly expands the high‐value chemical portfolio derivable from common plastic wastes. Incorporation of the synthesized DOXs into the poly(L‐lactic acid) (PLLA) backbone results in materials with enhanced thermal stability and superior biodegradability. Critically, this PLLA derivative outperforms both pristine PLLA and cellulose in degradation, marking the first reported instance of PLLA achieving such a result. This research moves beyond simple waste conversion, establishing a robust sustainable utilization strategy that transforms plastic upcycling products into functional monomers for next‐generation, superior biodegradable polymers.
item.content:encoded:
An acid-catalyzed process provides a robust method to upcycle even impure and colored PLA and POM plastic waste into high-value DOXs. Incorporating these DOXs enables the synthesis of PLLA materials that exhibit enhanced biodegradability, surpassing the performance of cellulose. This work establishes a sustainable chemical pathway and marks a significant step toward advanced materials.


Catalytic plastic upcycling protocols are essential for resolving the environmental and economic challenges associated with massive plastic production and disposal, as they convert complex waste streams into value-added chemicals. We report an efficient acid-catalyzed upcycling strategy for transforming poly(lactic acid) (PLA) and polyoxymethylene (POM) plastic waste. This innovative process directly yielding dioxolanones (DOXs) can be extended to use POM with various naturally abundant α-hydroxy acids. This work significantly expands the high-value chemical portfolio derivable from common plastic wastes. Incorporation of the synthesized DOXs into the poly(L-lactic acid) (PLLA) backbone results in materials with enhanced thermal stability and superior biodegradability. Critically, this PLLA derivative outperforms both pristine PLLA and cellulose in degradation, marking the first reported instance of PLLA achieving such a result. This research moves beyond simple waste conversion, establishing a robust sustainable utilization strategy that transforms plastic upcycling products into functional monomers for next-generation, superior biodegradable polymers.
item.dc:creator: Mi‐hyun Lee, Joohyun Park, Hyeongyeong Lee, Woo Yeon Cho, Semin Son, Pyung Cheon Lee, In‐Hwan Lee, Hye‐Young Jang
item.dc:title: Unveiling Value in Plastic Waste: Synthesis of 1,3‐Dioxolan‐4‐ones from Polylactic Acid and Polyoxymethylene to Enhance Plastic Biodegradation

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Palladium‐Catalyzed Defluorinative Chalcogenation of gem‐Difluorocyclopropanes
item.dc:description:
A palladium‐catalyzed ring‐opening chalcogenation of gem‐difluorocyclopropanes with selenols and thiols is reported. This approach provides an efficient and scalable route toward a series of value‐added 2‐fluoroallylic chalcogenides with remarkable regio‐ and stereoselectivity.
An unprecedented Pd(II)‐catalyzed defluorinative chalcogenation of gem‐difluorocyclopropanes has been developed. The coupling reactions of selenols (or their surrogates) and thiols with the strained fluorinated three‐membered ring lead to the formation of 2‐fluoroallylic chalcogenides with good regio‐ and stereoselectivity, which cannot be accessed through conventional approaches. This ring‐opening transformation exhibits remarkable atom economy and a broad substrate scope, offers suitability for scale‐up synthesis, and thereby provides a straightforward and promising avenue for the construction of chalcogen‐ and fluorine‐containing privileged scaffolds.
item.content:encoded:
A palladium-catalyzed ring-opening chalcogenation of gem-difluorocyclopropanes with selenols and thiols is reported. This approach provides an efficient and scalable route toward a series of value-added 2-fluoroallylic chalcogenides with remarkable regio- and stereoselectivity.


An unprecedented Pd(II)-catalyzed defluorinative chalcogenation of gem-difluorocyclopropanes has been developed. The coupling reactions of selenols (or their surrogates) and thiols with the strained fluorinated three-membered ring lead to the formation of 2-fluoroallylic chalcogenides with good regio- and stereoselectivity, which cannot be accessed through conventional approaches. This ring-opening transformation exhibits remarkable atom economy and a broad substrate scope, offers suitability for scale-up synthesis, and thereby provides a straightforward and promising avenue for the construction of chalcogen- and fluorine-containing privileged scaffolds.
item.dc:creator: Yu Teng, Yu‐Tao Xue, Meng‐Qiang Cai, Yi‐Dong Zhong, Hong‐Shuang Li
item.dc:title: Palladium‐Catalyzed Defluorinative Chalcogenation of gem‐Difluorocyclopropanes

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Metal‐Free Covalent Organic Frameworks for Photodegradation of Organic Contaminants in Water Environment
item.dc:description:
The design of metal‐free covalent organic frameworks (COFs)–based photocatalysts and their application for the degradation of the organic pollutions in water.
Effective removal of organic contaminants present in water is of vital importance for water remediation, public health, and economic benefits. Photodegradation has been recognized as a low‐energy consumption, recyclable, and mild treatment method for the rapid, efficient, and complete removal of organic contaminants. Among various photocatalytic materials, metal‐free covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have gained considerable attention to serve as photocatalytic materials in water remediation owing to their highly ordered structures, high specific surface areas, tunable pore environment, superior light absorption, and adjustable electronic structures. This article presents a comprehensive review of design strategies and applications of photocatalytic metal‐free COFs for organic contaminants degradation. Specifically, strategies for enhancing the photocatalytic performance of metal‐free COFs are discussed spanning from monomer design to framework construction in detail. Then, an analysis of reported metal‐free COFs for the photodegradation of organic contaminants is summarized. Finally, the current challenges and future opportunities for metal‐free COFs are highlighted. This review is expected to provide guidance for the rational design of photocatalytic metal‐free COFs and serve as a useful reference for future studies in water remediation.
item.content:encoded:
The design of metal-free covalent organic frameworks (COFs)–based photocatalysts and their application for the degradation of the organic pollutions in water.


Effective removal of organic contaminants present in water is of vital importance for water remediation, public health, and economic benefits. Photodegradation has been recognized as a low-energy consumption, recyclable, and mild treatment method for the rapid, efficient, and complete removal of organic contaminants. Among various photocatalytic materials, metal-free covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have gained considerable attention to serve as photocatalytic materials in water remediation owing to their highly ordered structures, high specific surface areas, tunable pore environment, superior light absorption, and adjustable electronic structures. This article presents a comprehensive review of design strategies and applications of photocatalytic metal-free COFs for organic contaminants degradation. Specifically, strategies for enhancing the photocatalytic performance of metal-free COFs are discussed spanning from monomer design to framework construction in detail. Then, an analysis of reported metal-free COFs for the photodegradation of organic contaminants is summarized. Finally, the current challenges and future opportunities for metal-free COFs are highlighted. This review is expected to provide guidance for the rational design of photocatalytic metal-free COFs and serve as a useful reference for future studies in water remediation.
item.dc:creator: Hongying Guo, Yang Li, Weiwei Huan, Jiandong Pang
item.dc:title: Metal‐Free Covalent Organic Frameworks for Photodegradation of Organic Contaminants in Water Environment

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: DMAP‐Promoted Dehalogenative Sulfonylation of 4,5‐Dihalo‐Pyridazin‐3(2H)‐Ones With Arylsulfonyl Hydrazides: An Approach to 4‐Arylsulfonylpyridazin‐3(2H)‐Ones
item.dc:description:
A dehalogenative sulfonylation of 4,5‐dihalo‐pyridazin‐3(2H)‐ones with sulfonyl hydrazides under mild conditions for the synthesis of 4‐arylsulfonyl pyridazin‐3(2H)‐ones is developed. The transformation avoids the use of transition‐metal catalysts, oxidants, and reductant and successfully introduces sulfonyl group at C‐4 site of pyridazin‐3(2H)‐ones.
A dehalogenative sulfonylation of 4,5‐dihalo‐pyridazin‐3(2H)‐ones with arylsulfonyl hydrazides under mild conditions for the synthesis of 4‐arylsulfonyl pyridazin‐3(2H)‐ones is developed for the first time. The transformation also avoids the use of transition‐metal catalysts, oxidants, and reductants. Notably, due to the relatively low activity of the C‐4 site of pyridazin‐3(2H)‐ones, there are few reports on functionalization reactions at this position before, especially on sulfonylation. Thus, this strategy is of great significance to expand both the synthetic range of potential functional pyridazin‐3(2H)‐one compounds and the application scope of arylsulfonyl hydrazines as a kind of multifunctional reagents.
item.content:encoded:
A dehalogenative sulfonylation of 4,5-dihalo-pyridazin-3(2H)-ones with sulfonyl hydrazides under mild conditions for the synthesis of 4-arylsulfonyl pyridazin-3(2H)-ones is developed. The transformation avoids the use of transition-metal catalysts, oxidants, and reductant and successfully introduces sulfonyl group at C-4 site of pyridazin-3(2H)-ones.


A dehalogenative sulfonylation of 4,5-dihalo-pyridazin-3(2H)-ones with arylsulfonyl hydrazides under mild conditions for the synthesis of 4-arylsulfonyl pyridazin-3(2H)-ones is developed for the first time. The transformation also avoids the use of transition-metal catalysts, oxidants, and reductants. Notably, due to the relatively low activity of the C-4 site of pyridazin-3(2H)-ones, there are few reports on functionalization reactions at this position before, especially on sulfonylation. Thus, this strategy is of great significance to expand both the synthetic range of potential functional pyridazin-3(2H)-one compounds and the application scope of arylsulfonyl hydrazines as a kind of multifunctional reagents.
item.dc:creator: Shi‐Wei Yu, Yu Zeng, Zhong‐Hao Li, Yong‐Tong Liang, Nan Huang, Ji‐Lin Guo, Chu‐Ming Pang, Zhao‐Yang Wang
item.dc:title: DMAP‐Promoted Dehalogenative Sulfonylation of 4,5‐Dihalo‐Pyridazin‐3(2H)‐Ones With Arylsulfonyl Hydrazides: An Approach to 4‐Arylsulfonylpyridazin‐3(2H)‐Ones

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Beyond the Classic Approach: Iodine(lll/V) Reagents Mediated Hofmann Rearrangement for Construction of Nitrogen‐Containing Complex Molecules
item.dc:description:
Hypervalent iodine chemistry has emerged as a revolutionary and environmentally benign platform for the classical Hofmann rearrangement, facilitating the in situ generation of highly reactive isocyanate intermediates. These versatile intermediates readily undergo efficient and selective reactions with diverse nucleophiles including water, amines, alcohols, and carboxylic acids under mild conditions. This article comprehensively surveys the significant progress achieved in Hofmann rearrangement reactions mediated by hypervalent iodine reagents, which aims to serve as a valuable reference for researchers engaged in the synthesis and application exploration of nitrogen‐containing compounds using this transformative methodology.
Hypervalent iodine chemistry has revolutionized the Hofmann rearrangement by providing an environmentally benign alternative to traditional hazardous reagents. Employing compounds such as PhI(OAc)2 and PhI(OCOCF3)2, this strategy enables mild activation of amides to generate reactive isocyanate intermediates in situ. These intermediates readily engage with a range of nucleophiles including water, amines, alcohols, and carboxylic acids under mild conditions, facilitating the one‐pot synthesis of diverse nitrogen‐containing products. Unlike the classical Hofmann rearrangement, which affords only primary amines, this method allows efficient access to ureas, urethanes, carbamates, and amides. Key advantages include improved atom economy, simplified synthetic procedures, broad functional group tolerance, and enhanced applicability to complex molecule synthesis. This article highlights recent advances in hypervalent iodine‐mediated Hofmann rearrangements, offering a valuable resource for researchers developing novel routes to nitrogenous compounds in pharmaceutical and materials chemistry.
item.content:encoded:
Hypervalent iodine chemistry has emerged as a revolutionary and environmentally benign platform for the classical Hofmann rearrangement, facilitating the in situ generation of highly reactive isocyanate intermediates. These versatile intermediates readily undergo efficient and selective reactions with diverse nucleophiles including water, amines, alcohols, and carboxylic acids under mild conditions. This article comprehensively surveys the significant progress achieved in Hofmann rearrangement reactions mediated by hypervalent iodine reagents, which aims to serve as a valuable reference for researchers engaged in the synthesis and application exploration of nitrogen-containing compounds using this transformative methodology.


Hypervalent iodine chemistry has revolutionized the Hofmann rearrangement by providing an environmentally benign alternative to traditional hazardous reagents. Employing compounds such as PhI(OAc)2 and PhI(OCOCF3)2, this strategy enables mild activation of amides to generate reactive isocyanate intermediates in situ. These intermediates readily engage with a range of nucleophiles including water, amines, alcohols, and carboxylic acids under mild conditions, facilitating the one-pot synthesis of diverse nitrogen-containing products. Unlike the classical Hofmann rearrangement, which affords only primary amines, this method allows efficient access to ureas, urethanes, carbamates, and amides. Key advantages include improved atom economy, simplified synthetic procedures, broad functional group tolerance, and enhanced applicability to complex molecule synthesis. This article highlights recent advances in hypervalent iodine-mediated Hofmann rearrangements, offering a valuable resource for researchers developing novel routes to nitrogenous compounds in pharmaceutical and materials chemistry.
item.dc:creator: Hui‐cheng Cheng, Jiao‐li Ma
item.dc:title: Beyond the Classic Approach: Iodine(lll/V) Reagents Mediated Hofmann Rearrangement for Construction of Nitrogen‐Containing Complex Molecules
